text:
RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TB1-11306
Private Proceeding / Huis clos
Reasons and Decision ? Motifs et Décision
Claimant(s)
XXXX XXXX
Demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
November 13, 2013
January 21, 2014
Date(s) of Hearing
June 2, 2014

Toronto, Ontario

October 30, 2014

and reasons
et des motifs

Edward C. Robinson
Tribunal
Counsel for the Claimant(s)
Steven Beiles
Barrister and Solicitor
Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile

N/A
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)

N/A
Conseil du (de la) ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
INTRODUCTION
[1] XXXX XXXX (the claimant) is a citizen of the Russian Federation (Russia)1 and an ethnic Chechen. He claims refugee protection pursuant to section 96 and subsection 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).2
ALLEGATIONS
[2] The claimant is a 26 year old male with 15 years education including a University degree in XXXX. His father was Chechen and his mother Russian. The claimant alleged that his father was harassed by the Russian government, FSB and the press. He alleged that in 1994 his father was murdered while on a business trip to Moscow and his death was not properly investigated by the authorities. The claimant alleged that when his father was killed he was in first grade; things got so bad that he had to attend school with bodyguards and his brother, XXXX was forced to leave Russia. His brother went to the US in 1997. While his brother was in the US officers from the FSB came to their home in 2003 looking for XXXX. They claimed his brother was fighting in Chechnya on the rebels' side.
[3] The claimant alleged that in 2009 he was attacked by local skinheads and beaten up. The police were called but never came. He was taken to the polyclinic for treatment. The police were notified and they attended and took his statement. The claimant alleged that he soon started to receive threatening telephone calls. The caller told him that he would not get protection from the police. The claimant alleged that he reported the threats to the police. The claimant alleged that in XXXX 2009 he was attacked again. The claimant was treated medically and reported the incident to the police. In XXXX 2009, the claimant alleged that he was attacked again. He ran and saw a police car he tried waving for them to stop. The police car stopped and the officer began swearing at him. The claimant alleged that he went to the police station and made a report. He received no help so he decided to make a report to the prosecutor's office citing police inaction and lack of protection but he never received any feedback from the prosecutor officer.
[4] In XXXX 2010, the claimant was attacked again one of whom he recognized from a previous attack. He was treated at the polyclinic and the doctor notified the police but they did not show up. In XXXX 2010, he was involved in an altercation with some Nationalists. The police arrived and recognized that he was Chechen and detained him and took him to the police station. On his way he was verbally and physically abused. They demanded he paid a bribe or else he would be locked in a cell. He alleged that he became afraid of the police as much as he was afraid of the nationalists.
[5] Fearing for his life and safety he and his girlfriend left Russia and came to Canada
DETERMINATION
[6] The claimant is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection.
ANALYSIS
Credibility & Subjective Fear
[7] With regard to credibility, the panel is guided by the Federal Court of Appeal which has ruled that testimony given under oath is presumed to be true, unless there is a valid reason to doubt its truthfulness.3 The assessment the panel must use to test the truth of a story of a witness is that it be in harmony with the preponderance of probabilities which a practical and informed person would readily recognize as reasonable in that place and in those conditions.4 Furthermore, the panel cannot be satisfied that, "the evidence is credible or trustworthy unless satisfied that it is probably so, not just possibly so."5
[8] The panel, in considering the credibility of the claimant, made an assessment of all the evidence, both oral and documentary.6 The evidence was also assessed as a whole so it could be treated in a consistent manner.7 However, not every piece of evidence will be referred to, but those the panel finds relevant to its decision.8 Even if a piece of evidence is not referred to, the panel carefully considered it as part of the evidence.9 The panel is entitled to make reasonable findings based on implausibility, common sense and rationality, and may reject evidence if it is not consistent with the probabilities affecting the case as a whole.
[9] The panel believes that the claimant is bi-racial, as his father was Chechen and his mother Russian. The panel also believes that the claimant's father was killed on XXXX XXXX, 199410 and that the claimant's brother was granted refugee status in XXXX 200411 and became XXXX citizens in 2009. The panel does not believe that the claimant had the kinds of problems he said he had in Russia because of his ethnicity and because of his father's alleged bloodline to XXXX XXXX, XXXX of the XXXX XXXX in Chechen. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant concocted an elaborate story of personal persecution and persecution of his family by the Russian authorities and nationalists to bolster his claim for refugee status. For example, the claimant has used a basic set of facts namely the unrest in Chechenya and the war for independence that broke out in December 1994 between Russia and Chechenya and through embellishment and exaggerations come up with his story of persecution.
Bloodline and Persecution of Claimant's Father
[10] The claimant claimed that his father was an influential person in the Chechen community of XXXX. This placed him under a lot of pressure from the Russian authorities. They wanted to manipulate the local Chechen population through him. He also testified that his father was "remotely related to XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX of the XXXX XXXX and the XXXX of the XXXX XXXX". His father was killed XXXX December 1994 by the Russian government because his father refused to cooperate in a smear campaign against XXXX XXXX. He said that XXXX XXXX portrayed his father as an accomplice of the Chechen XXXX XXXX and was detained for a week. However, the claimant said his father was given XXXX XXXX apology for the detention. His father however started to receive threats and in XXXX 1994 he was shot at, the bullet missed but the claimant's brother, XXXX was shot and wounded. He stated that the authorities did not properly investigate his father's murder.
[11] The panel examined the claimant's allegations that the Russian authorities killed his father and found that they were largely speculative. For example, the panel finds no reliable evidence that the claimant's father was killed by the Russian authorities and that his murder was not properly investigated. In fact, a XXXX XXXX indicates that his life was under constant threat by XXXX Moscow and Samara thugs including Chechen people as well who demanded that he forget about debts that were owed to the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. "XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX put a contract out on his life after he refused."12 This was not the Russian authorities. And the fact that so much information was provided in XXXX XXXX XXXX suggests that an indebt investigation was carried out. As such, the panel believes XXXX XXXX XXXX and finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant made up the story that his father was killed by the Russian authorities to bolster his claim which undermines his credibility. The panel prefers the documentary evidence over the claimant's testimony since they are drawn from a wide range of publicly accessible documents from reliable non-government and government organizations.
[12] For example, XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX stated that "Representatives of the Federal Service came to him [XXXX XXXX] from Moscow with request to appear on XXXX XXXX with exposure of the XXXX XXXX, promising major problems from law-enforcement authorities in case of the opposite"13. XXXX XXXX also confirmed the claimant's allegation that his father was XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. Thus, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the Russian authorities did harass the claimant's father after he refused to XXXX XXXX XXXX to denounce the XXXX XXXX. But there is no persuasive evidence that this harassment continued after the claimant's father's arrest which was personally ordered by XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX of the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. The article stated that "he was released with apologies."14 With respect to the claimant's father's killer, the article also stated that "It can be assumed, the people arrived, were Chechens, XXXX XXXX opened the door for them at once"15. The panel rejects the claimant's and his mother's accusation that XXXX XXXX was pressured by the FSB to attribute his father's death to "local underworld and Chechen insurgents"16. In this panel's view, the article was balanced and speaks as well to the authority's role. The panel therefore finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant's father was not targeted by the Russian authorities, and if he was not, then the claimant was not as well.
Family's Persecution
[13] The claimant wrote in his PIF narrative that "There was also evidence that the Russian authorities could be directly involved in his father's death, so our family and the Chechen community of XXXX hired a team of private investigators, who were unable to do their job due to mysteriously disappearing evidence and the resistance of officials, which could not be classified in any other way than a cover up. Our family also received threats and persistent advice to stop digging if we wanted to live"17. But he provided no such evidence to this panel other than his mother's letter in which she stated her husband's "friends hired private detectives to investigate into the murder. When we requested copies of the investigation documents, we were told that the responsible investigator was transferred into a different department with documents, bullet cartridge and other pieces of evidence being lost. One of the FSB top bureaucrats insisted on our stopping to pry into the investigation, saying otherwise we would risk our lives"18 While the claimant's mother makes these serious allegations, they provided no objective evidence to corroborate them, such as a report from the private investigators that she said was hired to look into the murder of her husband. One would expect a copy of the investigators' report since they were hired to conduct an investigation. We have the claimant's mother's letters that offers claims, speculations and statements against the authorities but except for letters from other family members, there is very little corroboration. For example, the panel notes that XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX19 XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, there is absolutely no mention of any investigation by any private investigators into his death. XXXX XXXX as the panel already pointed out is XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX from a reliable non-government organization. As such, the panel accepts this independently produced document over the claimant's testimony and his mother's letters which in the case of the claimant can be considered self-serving and a mother trying to support her son. The panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant's mother made up the story20 that her husband was killed by the Russian authorities and his friends hired a group of private investigators to look into the killing.
[14] At the same time the panel notes that the claimant presented medical reports of his brother - XXXX - including a photo of his left hand in what seems to be a 'cast'21. A number of G-mails indicating that his sister, XXXX, his brother, XXXX and his mother are having problems in Russia due to their Chechen ethnicity and association with Chechens .22 A careful examination of these documents, raise some concerns in the panel's mind as to their trustworthiness in terms of the information they convey. For example, the panel notes that there is absolutely no mention in any of the claimant's brother's medical reports that his condition, be it physical or psychological had anything to do with any attack on him by nationalists. The claimant simply explained it in the addendum to his narrative that his brother "had to lie about the real cause of his injures, as the doctors are required to report all the beatings to the police. He also feared for my mom and sister. XXXX was afraid for his safety, retaliation by the attackers, and unlawful actions of the authorities".23
[15] The panel finds his explanation unreasonably self-serving. There is no evidence presented that his brother knew any of the attackers. The claimant called them "Russian nationalists". In fact, the victim called them "three morons"24. The panel therefore finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant's brother wouldn't be able to say exactly who the attackers were, neither to the doctors or the police. If he knew them on a balance of probabilities he would have said so. And if he would not be able to say who the attackers, were on a balance of probabilities, he and his family would not be targeted for merely saying they were attacked by Russian nationalists or three morons who they could not otherwise identify. Since the claimant's brother 'lied' to the doctors as to how he came by his injures and he did notmake a complaint to the police, there is no medical or police reports to corroborate the allegation that his brother was attacked and physically assaulted by anyone. All we have are allegations from family members supporting other family members that they are being persecuted. For example, the claimant's mother speculated that her daughter may have been poisoned because she had a shivering temperature25 and her daughter saying that her mother is not feeling well because "she has been very nervous recently because of XXXX"26. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant's 'wife', his mother, his brothers and his sister have bonded together and through embellishments try to support the claimant's claim. As such, the panel assigns little weight to the claimant's mother's, brother's and sister's letters.
[16] The panel finds that the claimant's father suffered harassments at the hands of the Russian authorities and retaliation at the hands of the 'XXXX XXXX associated with the XXXX XXXX XXXX in XXXX and the latter who put a contract out on his life after he refused to XXXX XXXX XXXX"27 and who in turn killed him. The panel also notes that to the extent that the claimant's father was being harassed by the Russian authorities because of his refusal to cooperate with them in denouncing the actions of XXXX XXXX XXXX, the family would have been affected. But this was in the nineties, it is now 2014. The panel in examining the evidence before it finds no reliable and persuasive evidence before it that the claimant's family presently and in the recent past are being harassed by anyone in Russia. At the same time the panel is mindful that the claimant does not have to establish personal targeting or persecution or that he was persecuted in the past or will be persecuted in the future.28 The standard of proof in refugee claims is a serious possibility that the claimant would be persecuted if he returned to the country of origin.29 The Convention definition is forward looking and the fear of persecution is to be assessed at the time of the examination of the claim to refugee status.30
[17] There is evidence before this panel that racism and discrimination exists in Russia "towards anyone with non-Slavic features" and that "There is also a more sinister side to Russian racism, as evidenced by the multitude of attacks on immigrants in Moscow and across Russia"31. The panel notes that the claimant's family, with the exception of his mother, a Russian, is part Chechen. They were born in Russia, not Chechnya and speak Russian without an accent. The panel however notes that there is no independent corroborative evidence before this panel that the claimant and members of his family were persecuted in Russia because of their ethnicity.
Persecution vs Discrimination/Harassment
[18] To be found a Convention refugee, a claimant must have a well-founded fear of future persecution. A distinction can and should be made between persecution, on one hand, and discrimination or harassment on the other; with persecution being characterized by the greater seriousness of the mistreatment it involves.32 A finding of discrimination is within the jurisdiction of the Refugee Protection Division33 but persecution is defined in refugee law to be the inflicting of harm with repetition or persistence.34 The panel notes that cumulative acts of discrimination might indeed constitute persecution,35 in certain circumstances.
[19] The UNHCR Handbook on Procedures and Criteria for Determining Refugee Status notes that:
Differences in the treatment of various groups do indeed exist to a greater or lesser extent in many societies. People who receive less favourable treatment as a result of such differences are not necessarily victims of persecution. It is only in certain circumstances that discrimination will amount to persecution. This would be so if measures of discrimination lead to consequences of a seriously prejudicial nature for the person concerned, e.g. serious restrictions on his right to earn his livelihood, his right to practice his religion, or his access to normally available educational facilities.36
[20] In an attempt to demonstrate that cumulative acts of discrimination constituting persecution occurred, the claimant testified that three random incidents of violence against him, incidents that he has corroborative evidence for37, but has no corroborative evidence that they were indeed because of his ethnicity (half-Russian and half Chechen), amount to persecution. For example, the claimant stated that these incidents "didn't look spontaneous but rather a well prepared ambush"38 but he offered no persuasive evidence why he thought the incidents weren't spontaneous but a well prepared ambush. The panel therefore finds, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant at best was merely speculating that the incidents were planned and not spontaneous.
[21] The panel notes that the incidents for which there are medical reports to support occurred on XXXX XXXX, 2009, XXXX XXXX, 2009 and XXXX XXXX, 2010 respectively. The claimant however stated that there was another incident in XXXX 2009 in which he was attacked by skinheads. There is no corroborative evidence that this incident occurred and the panel does not believe it occurred. For example, the claimant stated that he went to the police station and made a report about the incident but the police were indifferent and unwilling to help. So he submitted a complaint to the prosecutor's office "citing among other things police inaction and lack of protection. I did notreceive any feedback from the prosecutor's Office".39 The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant made up the story that he was attacked and reported it to the local police who did nothing and he subsequently submitted a complaint to the prosecutor's office about police misconduct but received no feedback which undermines his credibility.
[22] For example, according to documentary evidence:
The Ministry of Internal Affairs, the Federal Security Service (FSB), and the Office of the Prosecutor General are responsible for law enforcement at all levels of government. The FSB is responsible for security, counterintelligence, and counterterrorism, and also for fighting crime and corruption. The national police force under the Ministry of Internal Affairs is organized at the federal, regional, and local levels. According to Yuriy Draguntsov, director general of security for the Ministry of Internal Affairs, 1,166 staff members were convicted of criminal misconduct in the first half of 2011, an increase of 25 percent from the same period the previous year. During this period, 35,000 officers (an increase of 9 percent) were registered in connection with crimes, and 60,500 crimes and infractions were committed (an increase of 21 percent). As of December 1, more than 10,000 employees (a 20 percent increase) were recommended for dismissal, while approximately 1,880 (a 19 percent increase) police officers were fired (Exhibit 3, Item 2.1, US Department of State (DOS) 2011 Country Reports on Human Rights Practices 25/05/2012 RUSSIA 43).
This shows that the government is taking steps to ensure that police officers are held accountable and the prosecutor's office is an integral part of this process of holding police officers accountable, and in the first half of 2011 over 1100 police officers were disciplined for misconduct. The panel believe the documentary evidence over the claimant's testimony since they are drawn from a wide range of publicly accessible documents from reliable non-government and government organizations.
[23] The only other incident of violence involving the claimant that the panel believes occurred is the one on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010. Since the panel does not believe that any other incidents occurred between XXXX 2009 and XXXX 2010 and between XXXX 2010 and XXXX 2010, as there is no corroborative evidence, except for XXXX testimony which the panel has impeached, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the incidents of violence against the claimant were random and he was not specifically targeted.
[24] At the same time, as stated before and perhaps worth mentioning again, the panel bears in mind that the claimant does not have to establish personal targeting or persecution or that he was persecuted in the past or will be persecuted in the future.40 The standard of proof in refugee claims is a serious possibility that the claimant would be persecuted if he returned to the country of origin.41 The Convention definition is forward looking and the fear of persecution is to be assessed at the time of the examination of the claim to refugee status.42
First Witness/XXXX XXXX
[25] The panel notes that the first witness testified she is the common-law wife of the claimant. She said that she has known the claimant since 2008 and that they came to Canada together. But when she was asked when they started the common-law relation she replied XXXX or XXXX last year - 2013. So, she was asked what kind of relationship they had before last year and she said "Same relationship, but we do taxes together now and we have our money at the same bank now". She then added that before "I would say we were dating". The panel finds that the witness seems unsure of the kind of relationship she has had with the claimant, especially when she was asked "When you came to Canada when did you start living together" and she said from the 1st day they came to Canada. That would be XXXX XXXX, 2010. The apparent lack of certainty about when their common-law relationship started gave rise to concerns by the panel.
[26] The panel therefore examined more closely the evidence that the witness and the claimant actually had a romantic relationship. The panel could find no persuasive evidence before it that the witness had a romantic relation much more a common-law relation with the claimant. For example, the evidence is that they lived together in Russia for a year before they changed their living arrangement when the witness went back home to live with her parents because she was told by campus security that they couldn't protect her. The decision stemmed from her door being vandalized - her surname was replaced with "I'm a Chechen". The witness, who like the claimant's mother, is ethnic Russian, is alleging that her door was vandalized because she and the claimant (half-Chechen) were living together.
[27] However, the witness testified that the claimant had his own residence on campus. As such, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that they were not living together on campus. They made the story up to suggest that she was also persecuted because she, a Russian woman, was with a Chechen. At the same time the panel notes that the claimant said that he graduated from XXXX in XXXX 2009. It is reasonable to assume that he would have moved off campus then and was living elsewhere because he wrote "In XXXX 2009 I had to move my residence after another skinhead attack which occurred near my house [panel's emphasis]43. The panel therefore finds on a balance of probabilities that they were not living together on campus.
[28] The evidence that the claimant was being persecuted because he was seeing a Russian woman is particularly important because the claimant is alleging that his mother was persecuted because she, an ethnic Russian, was in a relationship with his father, a Chechen and things have not changed because his girlfriend, an Ethnic Russian is also being persecuted because she is seeing a Chechen. However, in disbelieving the claimant and the witness that they were in a Romantic relation and were persecuted because of it, the panel notes that despite his not being ethnic Russian when it suited him to do so, he was able to hide his Chechen ethnicity. For example, he wrote "Even when I met my future girlfriend XXXX in the university in 2008 I was hesitant and waited for some time to tell her of my ethnic background, afraid of rejection. When I openly told her everything, XXXX did not reject me."44
[29] But, in an attempt to support the claimant's story of persecution based on his ethnicity, when the witness was asked by counsel how is her husband known as a Chechen. She replied that he does notlook like Russians. She explained that his eyes, nose, hair and cheekbones are all different. Yet, as the panel pointed out earlier, the claimant stated that for fear she would reject him he waited for some time before he told her about his ethnicity which seems to suggest that XXXX was not able to tell he was not Russian. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant retained this witness to support his fraudulent claim that he was persecuted because of his Chechen ethnicity and particularly because he got involved with a Russian girl.
[30] In finding that the witness is not the claimant's common-law wife, the panel notes that he indicated 'not applicable' to the question 3a, pg. 3 of his PIF45. The panel notes that the claimant is represented by counsel. Therefore if indeed the claimant had a common-law wife on a balance of probabilities he would not have stated 'not applicable' to the question of his marital status which specifically includes common-law relationship. Again, the panel notes that the witness testified that she has been living with the claimant from the first day they arrived in Canada. However, the claimant's amended PIF indicates that she is the claimant's common-law wife since July 1, 201346. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant and the witness are not in a common-law relationship and in particular they were not in a romantic relationship while they were in Russia. Their allegation that they were is to bolster the claimant's claim.
Second Witness/XXXX XXXX
[31] The panel notes that this witness is the claimant's brother. He left Russia in 1997 and went to the US where he remained until he left and came to Canada in 2003 and filed for refugee protection which was granted in 2006. In the nineties when the witness left his country, as the panel notes, the situation in Russia would have been different than it is now which makes the notion that each claim must be decided on its own merits significant because circumstances and situations in a country can change from one moment to the next. For example, the witness became a XXXX citizen in 2009 and when he was asked when was the last time he saw the claimant before the claimant came to Canada in 2010. The witness said it was in the summer of 2009. He said that he spent a week in Russia. But when he was asked if he was aware of his brother's problems while he was in Russia, he said he did not know about them. He said he learned about the problems about a year later.
[32] The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that if indeed the claimant was having problems in Russia the witness would have known about it when he visited Russia and saw his brother. He said he did not know even though the evidence before this panel indicates that they are a closely knitted family. For example, the claimant wrote that the first attack against him by skinheads occurred in XXXX 2009. They hit him from behind and he fell to the ground and they began kicking him and calling him derogatory names including racial slurs. That was in the winter. In XXXX of the same year he was attacked again. That was in the spring. And in XXXX he had to change his address because of another attack47. But while the claimant had allegedly experienced two attacks from "nationalistic bigots" because of his Chechen ethnicity before the summer of 2009, when his brother returned to Russia for a visit, the claimant didn't tell his brother anything about these attacks. Bear in mind the witness had also experienced discrimination when he lived in Russia, in the view of this panel, if indeed the claimant was being persecuted to the extent he said he was, and so far he had been attacked twice by nationalists, he would have told his brother. After all, his brother had gone through a similar experience. As such, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant did not tell his brother about the alleged assaults simply because the assaults were not because of his ethnicity. In other words, the claimant could not have told his brother in the summer of 2009 about any persecution because he was not being persecuted by Russian nationalists and he had not yet concocted that story to bolster his refugee claim.
Claimant's Alleged Persecution
[33] The claimant stated he was attacked and physically assaulted by nationalistic bigots and other racists three times in 2009 and three times in 2010. One of the incidents in 2010 was perpetrated by the police. The panel does not believe that he was attacked and assaulted by anyone in Russia including the police because of his Chechen ethnicity. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that he made up the story to enhance his refugee claim. For example, the claimant stated that he sought and obtained medical treatment for some of the assaults against him. He submitted a number of medical reports to corroborate these assaults [those he allegedly sought medical treatments for]48. According to the medical reports he was beaten by 'unknown individuals' on XXXX XXXX, 2009, XXXX XXXX, 2009 and XXXX XXXX, 2010. He also had follow-up consultations with a XXXX beginning on XXXX XXXX, 2009 to XXXX XXXX, 2010. The conclusion decision as stated in the last XXXX report is Posttraumatic syndrome: Depression disorder of strong degree.49
[34] If the medical reports are authentic (and the panel makes no finding that they are not), there is however no stated motivation for the attacks and assaults against the claimant in those reports. They simply state that he was beaten by unknown individuals. When the claimant was asked if he told the doctors that he was attacked by nationalist criminals he replied "I didn't say I was attacked by nationalists, I just said I was beaten". The panel finds his answer instructive. The claimant didn't inform the doctors that he was physically attacked because of his race or ethnicity which is what he wants the panel to believe was the reason he was attacked. However, all he told the doctors was that he was beaten. The panel therefore finds that if the claimant was indeed attacked and assaulted because of his Chechen ethnicity on a balance of probabilities he would have told the doctors so. He did not and the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the attacks and assaults against him had nothing to with his ethnicity or "nationality" which undermines his credibility.50
[35] The panel notes that the claimant has medical reports for the three incidents mentioned but he testified that he was attacked and injured subsequently by nationalists and the police in Moscow. But he provided no medical reports for these alleged incidents. The panel does not believe that he was attacked and assaulted subsequently to those three incidents just mentioned as well as the alleged XXXX 2009 incident. For example, he testified that he moved to Moscow on XXXX XXXX, 2010 to escape the violence against him in his home town. Bear in mind that this is the same month he went to Moscow to apply for his Canadian visa. In fact, the claimant testified that he remained in Moscow until he left Russia on XXXX XXXX, 2010. He testified that in Moscow he observed various atrocities committed by nationalists and the police against racial minorities. He said "I tried to exercise extreme caution, limit my trips to the city and not come out after dark at all".51 The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant went to Moscow to apply for his visa not because he was escaping persecution in his hometown as he alleged.
[36] For example, the claimant testified that he applied to the Canadian Embassy in Moscow for a student visa in XXXX 2010 and he received a negative response the following month. He submitted another application in XXXX 2010 and received a positive response in XXXX 2010. He didn't leave right away because he waited for XXXX to get her visa. He said he would not leave without her. The claimant provided no documentary evidence to corroborative his testimony that he waited for XXXX and that they traveled together to Canada. The claimant also testified that in XXXX 2010 he was walking with XXXX in Moscow when a group of nationalists who asked her why such a pretty Russian girl, like her was with a "monkey from Caucasus". The men started to attack and assault the claimant while XXXX approached a police patrol that had arrived and asked for help. The police intervened and asked them for IDs. He showed his passport and when the police saw that he was Chechen they confiscated his passport, let the attackers go and escorted the claimant to the police station. But on their way to the police station the claimant was physically and verbally abused by the police. They also demanded that he pay a bribe to avoid being locked up. He testified that he was released by the police between 7:30 and 9pm. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that these incidents did not occur.
[37] For example, seeing that the claimant said that he was physically assaulted first by the nationalists and then by the police he was asked if he sought medical attention for the incidents and he said no. Asked why not and he said "Caused I didn't have registration in Moscow; didn't have money, didn't have anyone in Moscow; I was depressed. I called XXXX, I went home". The panel finds his explanation unreasonable. He seems to be making up the story as he went along. For example, he said his mother was sending him money and he had been working on his job from home. The panel also reminded the claimant that he wrote in his PIF narrative that he sought medical help at a private clinic in Moscow and he agreed. Asked if he had that medical report and he said no. Asked why not and he explained that "I just had a headache; I just needed my medication, so they didn't give me any documents".
[38] The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant made up the entire story of his problems in Moscow including that he was attacked and assaulted first by nationalists and then by the police but he didn't seek medical treatment because he didn't have any money and Moscow registration. After all he went to seek medical treatment at a private clinic when he needed to. If he was attacked and assaulted by anyone on a balance of probabilities he would have at least sought medical treatment and requested a medical report. The claimant was asked if he had any independent evidence that he was attacked by the police and others in Moscow. He said "I have a photograph; I don't have a medical report". The panel finds his answer instructive. In disbelieving the claimant that he was assaulted and XXXX provided first aid service to him as he claims, the panel reminds itself that when the claimant was asked "Don't you think that if you had a medical report it would be helpful to your case?" He replied "I don't know". Yet, even though he said that he did not know if a medical report would be helpful to his case, he presented a photograph purporting to show his nose bleeding52 expecting the photograph to be helpful to his case. In examining the photograph the panel cannot be satisfied that what appears on the photo is indeed a nose bleed. The panel therefore assigns very little weight to the photo.
[39] The claimant testified that XXXX helped him by disinfecting the wound with a substance and getting painkiller from the pharmacy. A careful examination of the evidence before this panel, leads it to find on a balance of probabilities that XXXX was not in the claimant's life when he was in Moscow, in XXXX or at university. For example, she is not mentioned in any of the correspondence between the claimant, his mother, sister and brother in Russia. No where did the panel note anyone asking how the claimant's wife or even girlfriend was doing or sending a hello to her53. But even more important, one of the times when the claimant was asked if he had sought medical attention, he said no but quickly added "XXXX helped me". He also said that it was her idea to take the photograph with his alleged nose bleed. When he was asked what was the reason she took the photo. He said that she told him she was going to ask the police to help them. But he admitted that they did not take the photo to the police. Since according to him the police were also responsible for assaulting him, he was asked if he knew which branch of the police she was going to take the photo to and he said he didn't know.
[40] The panel also notes that the claimant testified that in XXXX 2010 he left Russia for the Czech Republic to escape the violence in Russia. He said that XXXX went with him as well to the Czech Republic, then back to Russia and then to Canada. In fact, when XXXX testified in support of the claimant's claim she was asked "What was your purpose for coming to Canada". She said to study and to save the claimant's life. Not understanding what she meant by 'to save the claimant's life', she was asked to explain. She replied "For it wasn't safe for him to stay in Russia". She was asked to explain what she meant and she replied that he was beaten up several times in Russia and he could die" Asked who had beaten him up several times and she replied nationalists and added "because he is Chechen". The panel finds her explanation unreasonable. The panel still does not understand how her coming to Canada saved the claimant's life.
[41] XXXX testified that she reported the vandalism to her door so the panel asked if she had submitted a written statement to the police about her vandalized door she said no. Asked why not and she said "I don't know, I just went to tell the police what happened". The panel finds her answer vague. Asked if she signed a statement at the police station and she said that she didn't remember. And when she was asked what she told the police at the station. She said "I told them my door was vandalized by people who attacked my boyfriend, so it is probably not safe for me". Asked if the police wrote down what she told them and she said "I don't remember, I think they didn't take it seriously". She was asked if the police came and took a look at the door and she said no. She was asked to explain why she can't remember whether or not the police wrote down her report, or if she signed her statement, but she can remember that they didn't come out to look at her door. She replied, "I remember some things and I don't remember some things". The panel finds her explanation unreasonable. If she can remember that the police didn't come out and take a look at her door it is reasonable for the panel to expect that she would also remember if she signed a statement or they took her report in writing. As such, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that she didn't make any complaint to the police. And she didn't because he door was not vandalized, and if it was not vandalized it is because she didn't have a relationship with the claimant.
[42] In examining the evidence before it, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant and the witness did not give trustworthy testimony on which the panel can rely.
Psychological Report
[43] The claimant testified that he was depressed in Russia as well as when he came to Canada and he presented a psychological report54 According to the report he has been diagnosed to be afflicted by Symptoms of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder as well as Major Depressive Disorder Moderate. It is the practitioner's opinion the claimant is highly vulnerable to a psychological decompensation and collapse, if he or his partner must return to Russia, where he perceives that they will be harmed and likely killed. The panel didn't observe any manifestations to suggest that the claimant was in any adverse psychological condition at the hearing. Whatever the claimant's mental or psychological state is, the panel in examining the evidence before it does not believe it was as a result of persecution in Russia. The panel notes the Board has been upheld by the courts in giving very little weight to a report prepared by a practitioner attesting to the claimant's veracity when it did not find the claimant credible55. The claimant testified that he had one visit with Dr. XXXX XXXX. When the claimant was asked how long was the session with the doctor, he replied "One hour for sure, but maybe more". Counsel then informed the panel that Dr. XXXX was in possession of the claimant's neurological reports from Russia.
Delay in Claiming
[44] The evidence before the panel is that the claimant left Russia on XXXX XXXX, 2010 and came to Canada. When he was asked when did he file for refugee protection he said July 2011. The panel notes that he signed his PIF on August 24, 2011. Whether it is July or August it is clear that he claimed refugee protections several months after he arrived in Canada. The claimant was asked why he waited so long to file for refugee protection. He replied that after he arrived in Canada he was depressed and he was not in a condition to make any decision himself. The plan was that he came to Canada to study. He would go to school for two years and then work for a year after which he would apply for his permanent residence in Canada. He said that he followed his brother's advice who told him that the only correct way was to go through the process is through the student program. He said he trusted his brother totally for he was not in a position to do it himself. He added that with the abuse of his family and his being beaten and abused in Russia it took him some time for him to get back to normal and "then time passed".
[45] The claimant testified that he applied for the renewal of his study visa. XXXX applied as well but she received her renewal and he did not. He said that he panicked because he didn't want to be sent back to Russia. So he approached the Canadian authorities and filed for refugee protection. He testified that he started XXXX class in January 2011 but even though he received help from XXXX and XXXX he still had lots of difficulties. He was asked if had stopped going to school and he replied "In July 2011". Asked why he had stopped going to school and he said "Because I applied for refugee protection and my program was cancelled". The panel finds the claimant's explanation as to why he waited so long to file for refugee protection to be unsatisfactory which undermines his credibility of a subjective fear.
[46] If indeed he was having problems, be it psychological or otherwise, this is Canada, there is help available. He was not alone in this country. He said he has his common-law wife as well as his brother and his brother's wife. He admitted that his brother suggested that he seeks help but he said that he 'didn't want anybody digging into his head". He didn't seek psychological help to deal with his problems relative to his studies and schooling but he went to see a psychologist to assist him in his refugee claim. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that if he indeed had a subjective fear he would have sought refugee protection sooner.
[47] The panel rejects his brother's allegation that he encouraged the claimant not to file for refugee protection to spare him the suffering that he went through when he sought refugee protection. While the panel believes that the refugee process here in Canada can be stressful on a claimant, it does not believe with the support systems available, including psychological help, it is unduly stressful to the extent the claimant needed to be protected from it.
[48] In the Singh56 decision Mr. Justice Shore said in part:
There's a well-established principle to the effect to any person having a well-founded fear of persecution should claim refugee protection in Canada as soon as he or she arrives in the country if that is his or her intent. On this point the Federal Court of Appeal has already concluded that delay in claiming refugee protection is an important factor which the Board may take into consideration in its analysis. Such a delay indicates a lack of subjective fear of persecution since there is an assumption to the effect that a person having a well-founded fear of persecution will claim refugee protection at the first opportunity. Accordingly in conducting its assessment the Board is entitled to take into consideration the applicant's delay in claiming refugee protection.
[49] In the Nijjar57 decision, Mr. Justice de Montigny declared:
It is trite law that the delay in submitting your refugee protection claim while not decisive remains a relevant element that the Tribunal may take into account in assessing both the statements and the actions and deeds of a claimant The claimant knew upon his arrival that he was only authorized to stay in Canada for a specific and limited period of time. Under these circumstances it was reasonable to expect that he regularize this status as soon as possible if he truly feared for his life and physical integrity.
[50] Similarly in the case of Assadi Nasser-Eddin58 the court held: "Failure to immediately seek protection can impugn the claimant's credibility including his or he testimony about events in his country of origin."
[51] The delay in filing, in the panel's opinion, may not have been very long but nonetheless long, and his explanation is unreasonable which undermines his credibility of a subjective fear. The panel therefore determines that the claimant's delay of roughly seven months in seeking Canada's protection is not consistent with subjective fear of persecution in Russia.
[52] The panel notes the various documents that the claimant presented in support of his claim59, the panel is mindful that documentary evidence may satisfy the objective requirement of his fear, but it cannot provide proof of a subjective fear, which must come from the claimant.60 The Federal Court has also found that where a claimant is found to be lacking in credibility, the panel can legitimately find that there is no subjective fear basis for the claim. In such cases, it is very difficult, if not impossible, to find credible evidence of a claimant's subjective fear, notwithstanding the existing evidence of human rights violations in the claimant's country.61 Despite the documentary evidence, the oral testimony and counsel's attempt to prove otherwise in his submissions, this panel finds no basis of a subjective fear in this claim
[53] The panel has also considered the case of Sheikh which states:
... even without disbelieving every word [a claimant] has uttered, a...panel may reasonably find him so lacking in credibility that it concludes there is no credible evidence relevant to his claim... In other words, a general finding of a lack of credibility on the part of the [claimant] may conceivably extend to all relevant evidence emanating from his testimony. 62
State Protection
[54] Regarding the issue of state protection, there is a presumption that the state is capable of protecting its citizens, except in situations where the state is in complete breakdown. To rebut the presumption of state protection, a claimant must provide "clear and convincing" evidence of the state's inability to protect its citizens.
[55] Refugee protection is meant to be a form of surrogate protection to be invoked only in those situations where the refugee claimant has unsuccessfully sought the protection of their home state. The onus is on the claimant to approach the state for protection in situations where it might be reasonably forthcoming.63
[56] The documentary evidence does not indicate that Russia is a state in complete breakdown but does indicate that Russia continues to experience various human rights problems including restrictions on civil liberties.
The Russian Federation has a highly centralized political system, with power increasingly concentrated in the president, and a weak multiparty political system. The bicameral Federal Assembly consists of a lower house (State Duma) and upper house (Federation Council). Presidential elections in March featured accusations of government interference and manipulation of the electoral process. Security forces generally reported to civilian authorities; however, in some areas of the Northern Caucasus, there were serious problems with civilian control.
The most significant human rights problems during the year involved:
1. Restrictions of Civil Liberties: Following increased mobilization of civil society and mass demonstrations in reaction to elections, the government introduced a series of measures limiting political pluralism. During the year Russia adopted laws that impose harsh fines for unsanctioned meetings; identify nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) as "foreign agents" if they engage in "political activity" while receiving foreign funding; suspend NGOs that have U.S. citizen members or receive U.S. support and are engaged in "political activity" or "pose a threat to Russian interests"; recriminalize libel; allow authorities to block Web sites without a court order; and significantly expand the definition of treason. Media outlets were pressured to alter their coverage or to fire reporters and editors critical of the government.
2. Violations of Electoral Processes: Domestic and international observers described the presidential campaign as skewed in favor of the ruling party's candidate, Vladimir Putin. Procedural irregularities marred voting, with reports of vote fraud, administrative measures disadvantaging the opposition, and pressure on election monitoring groups. Several gubernatorial elections in October were likewise criticized.
3. Administration of Justice: Due process was denied during the detentions and trials of protesters arrested following the May 6 demonstration in Moscow in which a small group of the protestors engaged in violence; in the detention, trial, and sentencing of the members of the punk rock group Pussy Riot, who were charged with hooliganism motivated by religious hatred; and searches and criminal cases lodged against several political activists. Individuals responsible for the deaths of prominent journalists, activists, and whistleblowers, notably Sergey Magnitskiy, have yet to be brought to be brought to justice.
Other problems reported during the year included: allegations of torture and excessive force by law enforcement officials; life-threatening prison conditions; interference in the judiciary and the right to a fair trial; abridgement of the right to privacy; restrictions on minority religions; widespread corruption; societal and official intimidation of civil society and labor activists; limitations on the rights of workers; trafficking in persons; attacks on migrants and select religious and ethnic minorities; and discrimination against and limitation of the rights of lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender (LGBT) persons.64
[57] The claimant stated in his PIF narrative that he was physically and verbally attacked and assaulted by nationalists and skinheads and on one occasion was also physically assaulted by police officers. Some of these incidents were reported to the police but they never carried out any investigations. Other incidents were not reported to the police because those that were reported were not investigated. He said he knew for sure that the police wouldn't help. The claimant also stated he made a complaint to the prosecutor's office about the police failure to take and investigate his complaint and provide protection. But according to the claimant he didn't get any feedback from the prosecutor's office. The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the police took his reports.
[58] The panel notes that according to the claimant's three medical reports regarding to the incidents that occurred in XXXX and XXXX 2009 and XXXX 2010, the police were notified. There is no persuasive evidence before this panel that they were any other incidents including the claimant's allegation that he made a report to the prosecutor's office about police indifference to his complaints. The claimant testified that the prosecutor's office didn't respond to his complaint. Given the credibility concerns with the claimant's story and a lack of corroborative evidence before this panel that he made a complaint to and the prosecutor's office didn't provide any feedback to the claimant's complaint, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that he didn't make any complaint to the prosecutor's office.
[59] For example, there is evidence of the Russian authority taking steps to address the problem of police misconduct. For example in an article of the Human Rights Watch titled Russia: A Move to Investigate Police Abuses dated April 19, 2012 speaks about "The creation of a special unit to investigate crimes by law enforcement officials."65
[...] according to documentary evidence, there is the creation of a special unit to investigate crimes by law enforcement officials is an important step toward ending impunity and eliminating torture by law enforcement and security agencies in Russia Human Rights Watch said today. It will be critically important for the unit to have the independence, robust mandate authority, and resources it needs to do its job, Human Rights Watch said.66
[60] The panel also notes that actions are not only taken at the legislative level but at the operational level as well,
According to the chief of the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior, 2,156 crimes committed by policemen had been solved in the first five months of 2010, which is about 6.5 per cent more than in the same period in 2009.67 [As well],
The 2010 report by Global Integrity, an organization that provides public and private sectors with expertise on fighting corruption, citizens can file complaints against the police with the Prosecutor General's Office, the Federal Security Service and the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior. As well, in correspondence sent to the Research Directorate on 17 August 2011, the director of the Public Verdict Foundation states that the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior and the Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation are the organizations that receive complaints against police officers. The Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior carries out investigations and decides the penalties to be imposed in cases of disciplinary infractions. The Investigative Committee of the Russian Federation, which was separated from the "procuracy" and is now an independent organization, processes complaints about crimes committed by police. According to Global Integrity, citizens can file complaints via the Internet site of the Ministry of the Interior by filling out a form online. As well, various NGOs, like the Public Verdict Foundation, help citizens file complaints against the police.
[61] In June 2010, chief of the Department of Internal Security of the Ministry of the Interior stated that "'[i]n 2009 alone, over 27,000 citizens submitted complaints to internal security subunits' [of the Internal Security Department of the Ministry of the Interior]." In 2010, the number of complaints had increased by 50 percent. According to the chief, " '[i]n five months, 41,000 offenses committed by policemen have been exposed,'" which represents a 16 percent increase compared with the same period in 2009. The chief added that more than 2,000 crimes committed by policemen had been exposed. Global Integrity did not specify the nature of the complaints made against the police or of the crimes committed by them. The Paris daily newspaper Le Figaro reported on 22 February 2010 that, according to the president of Russia, 15,000 complaints of corruption were made against the police in 2009, which is only the " 'tip of the iceberg.'"68
[62] The panel believes and so finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant may have made three complaints to the police after the doctors notified them. But because, as the medical reports attest, the assailants were unknown and the claimant could not identify them there was not much the police could have done. The Court has said that random assaults such as those suffered by the [claimants], where the assailants are unknown to the victim and there are no independent witnesses are also difficult to effectively investigate and protect against. In all such circumstances, even the most effective, well-resourced and highly motivated police forces will have difficulty providing effective protection. This Court should not impose on other states a standard of "effective" protection that police forces in our own country, regrettably, sometimes only aspire to.69 The Court has also stated that it is not reasonable for a claimant to expect the police to seek out and arrest the claimant's persecutors when they did not provide their identities or any leads for the police.70
[63] The panel rejects the claimant's allegation that he was assaulted by the police, especially since he did not complain to the prosecutor's office about this assault. He alleged that almost a year previously he had complained to the prosecutor's office about police inaction and got no response but he did not complain about the alleged actual police assault against his person. He still had a responsibility to exhaust all reasonable available means in his own country before seeking international remedy. He agrees with the panel that he is an educated man. He has 15 years education including a university degree71. As such, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that if indeed he was assaulted by police officers he would have complained to the prosecutor's office. He is very much aware of this office. While the panel does not believe that he had approached the prosecutor's office about what he called "police inaction and lack of protection", even if he did, which would have been almost a year before, he should have approached the prosecutor's office in this case because with the previous incidents the assailants were unknown. In this instance, the police were involved and there are special mechanisms in place to deal with police misconduct.
[64] Not only is protection offered by police agencies to be considered but other state agencies as well.72 Protection can be provided not just by state-run agencies such as the police but also by non-governmental agencies which receive state funding.73 Some Federal Court decisions, however, hold a contrary view.74 The panel however notes that some Federal Court decisions have expressed reservations in this regard, however, stating that it is the police force that has the primary responsibility to protect a nation's citizens and, unless there is evidence to the contrary, alternative institutions do not constitute avenues of protection per se.
[65] The panel acknowledges that Russia continues to struggle with violence against minorities:
Human rights organizations, media sources and the United Nations (UN) Committee for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination (CERD), report increased racial discrimination, xenophobia, and racially motivated violence against ethnic minorities in Russia. Potential targets of violence include people from the Caucasus, Central Asia, and Siberia, immigrants from Africa, and Asia, Roma, Meshketian Turks and other ethnic minorities. According to the SOVA Center for Information and Analysis, a Moscow-based non-profit organization which monitors hate crimes, anyone with "non-Slavic features" may be assaulted by racists in Russia.75
[66] As well, certain groups in Russia continue to engage in:
Discrimination, Intolerance and Anti-Semitism: Chauvinist groups also have stepped up their campaign, including death threats, against those who defend the rights of religious and ethnic minorities and migrants. Russian police, particularly in Moscow, have increased efforts to combat violent hate crimes and, according to SOVA Center, violence has decreased three-fold since 2008. Local authorities often fail to investigate hate crimes and "skinhead" attacks, against Muslim Central Asians and migrants. Jewish community leaders told USCIRF that although anti-Semitism still exists in Russia, the Russian government no longer supports it. President Putin donated one month's salary towards Moscow's Jewish Museum of History and Tolerance Center, which opened in November 2012.76
[67] The panel is, however, presented with insufficient evidence that police services are not adequate. The panel is not obliged to provide proof that a state can offer its citizens effective protection, rather, the claimant bears the legal burden of rebutting the presumption that adequate state protection exists by adducing clear and convincing evidence which satisfies the RPD on a balance of probabilities.77 The claimant in this case has not done so. The panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, that state protection is adequate in Russia for the claimant.
[68] Based on the evidence, the panel finds there is no serious possibility that the claimant would be persecuted or would be subjected, on a balance of probabilities, to a danger of torture or to a risk to life or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment in Russia.
CONCLUSION
[69] The panel finds that, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant has failed to rebut the presumption of state protection with clear and convincing evidence and, as the issue of state protection is applicable to both sections 96 and 97 of the IRPA, his claim must fail.
[70] The panel concludes that the claimant is not a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection and rejects his claim.
(signed)
"Edward C. Robinson"
Edward C. Robinson
October 30, 2014
